shower.thought: We spend the first half of our lives learning who we are, and the second half unlearning it.
The only time you experience the present is when you stop thinking about it.
You'll never know if you're colorblind because you have no reference point for what other people actually see.
Every conversation you have is with a version of that person that only exists in your mind.
Nostalgia is just grief for the present moment, delayed.
The reason you can't tickle yourself is the same reason you can't surprise yourself—your brain already knows what you're doing.
Time moves slower when you're waiting for something because you're experiencing it twice: once in anticipation and once in reality.
You've probably had the best day of your life and didn't realize it at the time.
The hardest part of quitting something isn't stopping—it's becoming someone who doesn't do it anymore.
Confidence is just deciding that other people's judgment doesn't matter before they've even judged you.
Every word you've forgotten you ever knew is still somewhere in your brain, just locked behind a door you've lost the key to.
You can't actually see the color you're looking at right now—you only see what your brain decided it should be.
The people who make you feel understood are just people whose confusion matches yours.
Pain is the only thing that forces you to live entirely in the present moment.
When you dream, you're experiencing events that feel completely real to a version of you that has no ability to fact-check.
The reason we get bored is that our brains are too efficient—boredom is just your mind running out of new problems to solve.
You know you're getting old when your memories start feeling like stories someone told you.
The scariest thing about the future isn't that it's uncertain—it's that it will feel as normal as today does.
Bad habits are just efficient solutions to problems you're not aware you're solving.
You've spent your entire life being the main character of a story no one else can ever fully understand.

motivational.thought: The difference between who you are and who you want to be is what you do when nobody's watching. — Unknown
Every master was once a beginner who refused to give up. — Unknown
You can't control the wind, but you can adjust your sails. — Dolly Parton
Regret for the things we did is temporary; regret for the things we didn't do is permanent. — Unknown
The only person you should try to be better than is the person you were yesterday. — Unknown
Success is not final, failure is not fatal: it is the courage to continue that counts. — Winston Churchill
You don't rise to the level of your goals; you fall to the level of your systems. — Unknown
The man who moves a mountain begins by carrying away small stones. — Confucius
Your limitations are only as real as you believe them to be. — Unknown
Discipline is choosing between what you want now and what you want most. — Unknown
The only way to do great work is to love what you do. — Steve Jobs
Everything you want is on the other side of fear. — Jack Canfield
You are not behind. You are not lost. You are exactly where you need to be, learning what you need to learn. — Unknown
The quality of your life is determined by the quality of the questions you ask yourself. — Unknown
Start before you're ready. Everything you need will show up along the way. — Unknown
A smooth sea never made a skilled sailor. — Franklin D. Roosevelt
The person who says it cannot be done should not interrupt the person doing it. — Chinese Proverb
You don't need permission to become who you're meant to be. — Unknown
Pressure is just a privilege in disguise. — Unknown
Your struggle is not your shame; it's your strength in training. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
I told my girlfriend she'd be perfect if she just changed everything about herself.
She said, "I'm already doing that—it's called leaving you."

My therapist asked about my childhood trauma.
I said, "Let's not waste time on ancient history when I can tell you about my recent stuff."
I'm not saying my family is toxic, but our group chat has a warning label.

My dad said, "Son, one day you'll understand why I left."
I said, "Yeah, by meeting you."

I asked the crematorium if they offered a loyalty program.
They said, "Every tenth customer goes free."

My friend said he was going to therapy to work on himself.
I said, "Good luck—I've met you, and that's a lot of work."

I tried to join a support group for people with low self-esteem.
They said I wasn't qualified enough.

My psychiatrist told me to stop being so pessimistic.
I said, "That's not going to work."
He said, "See? That's the spirit."

I told my boss I needed mental health days.
He said, "Take as many as you want—nobody's noticed you working anyway."

My girlfriend asked if I was seeing someone else.
I said, "Only when I look in the mirror, and honestly, I'm not impressed."

I asked my accountant if I had any money left after taxes.
He said, "Define 'money.'"

My neighbor asked why I moved my bed to face the wall.
I said, "Better lighting when I'm staring at my life choices."

I told my doctor I think I'm dying.
He said, "We all are, just at different rates."

My mom asked if I was eating enough vegetables.
I said, "Does regret count as a vegetable?"

I got kicked out of my meditation class for "negative thinking."
Apparently, "this is pointless" wasn't the vibe they were going for.

My friend said, "You'd be so much happier if you just stayed positive."
I said, "I am—I'm positively certain everything will disappoint me."

I asked the dating app why I had no matches.
It said, "We checked, and you don't exist."

My therapist said I need to work on my anger management.
I said, "I AM WORKING ON IT," and apparently that wasn't the tone they meant.

I told my teacher I couldn't do my homework because of depression.
He said, "So could everyone else in 1997, but we still showed up."

My doctor said, "You need to exercise more and eat less."
I said, "I'll do both the moment I develop the will to live."
